findings.title: mapFTSToAnnual annualOCF/annualFCF arrays are not year-aligned with each other or annualRev
findings.description: mapFTSToAnnual uses two separate loops with conditional pushes that break positional alignment. In the first loop (income-statement rows), rows with rev==null are skipped via `continue`, so annualRev may have fewer entries than the input row count. In the second loop (cash-flow rows), annualOCF pushes only `if (op != null)` and annualFCF pushes only `if (fcf != null)`. Because the two loops iterate over independent FTS responses (annualFin vs annualCash), there is no guarantee they cover the same fiscal years, AND within the cashRows loop OCF and FCF can have different lengths. Bug #26 fix only patched _ftsExtractByYear (used for SBC/Capex/RnD), not this function. F-DP-021 noted the OpInc/GP/NI misalignment which the code addressed by pushing null placeholders for those — but the cash-flow side and the cross-array year alignment between rev and ocf/fcf was never fixed.
findings.mechanism: Suppose Yahoo returns 5 years of annualFin (years 2020-2024) and 4 years of annualCash (2021-2024, missing 2020). annualRev → 5 entries (after rev-skip). annualOCF → 4 entries. annualFCF → 4 entries (assuming op+capex both present). Methods doing `annualFCF[i] / annualRev[i]` compute year-mismatched FCF margin (e.g. FCF[0]=2024 vs Rev[0]=2024, but FCF[3]=2021 vs Rev[3]=2021 only by luck — when 2020 in annualFin is dropped because rev is null but annualCash 2020 is also missing, alignment holds; when 2020 annualFin rev IS present and 2020 annualCash is missing, alignment breaks at index 0).
findings.reproducer: Pick a recent IPO (e.g. 2-3 year history) where Yahoo's annualFin has 4 years but annualCash has only 3. annualRev.length=4, annualFCF.length=3, methods doing year-aligned ratios silently misalign.
findings.impact: Year-misaligned fcf-margin, ocf-growth, fcf-yield computations. Affects fcf-yield-method, capex-discipline, cash-conversion methods. Silent corruption — no flag in snapshot.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) push null placeholders for missing rev rows so annualRev length matches input row count, then push null for OCF/FCF when their values are null too; or (b) preserve {year, value} tags and align by year in consumer methods; or (c) build a single year-indexed dictionary first, then emit aligned arrays.

findings.title: _ftsExtractByYear length can exceed annualRev — annualSBC/Capex/RnD misalign with annualRev when rev=null skipped
findings.description: _ftsExtractByYear was fixed (Bug #26 / FTS_CACHE_VERSION=2) to push null placeholders for missing fields, preserving row count. But it iterates the input rows verbatim with no skipping. mapFTSToAnnual, in contrast, skips entire rows where totalRevenue==null (line 466: `if (rev == null) continue;`). The result: annualRev.length can be smaller than annualSBC/annualCapex/annualRnD.length (which are produced by _ftsExtractByYear on the same fts.annualCash/fts.annualFin source). Cross-array indexing by `i` becomes year-misaligned. Methods computing SBC/Revenue or Capex/Revenue divide by the wrong year's revenue.
findings.mechanism: If fts.annualCash has 5 rows but row index 4 (i.e., 2020) has rev=null in annualFin: annualRev pushed 4 entries (skipping 2020), but annualCapex from _ftsExtractByYear pushed 5 (including null at the 2020 slot). canonical.annual.annualRev[0]=2024 aligns with annualCapex[0]=2024 only if both arrays are latest-first AND both omit 2020 — but only mapFTSToAnnual omits null-rev rows; _ftsExtractByYear keeps them. So annualRev[3]=2021 misaligns with annualCapex[3]=2021's REAL capex (which is at index 3 in the preserved-null array, possibly representing a different year).
findings.reproducer: Construct fts where annualFin has 5 rows with year[4].totalRevenue=null and 1-3 populated, but annualCash has 5 rows all populated. Inspect canonical.annual.annualRev.length (=4) vs annualCapex.length (=5). Method using ix=0..3 hits misalignment at the boundary.
findings.impact: sbc-revenue-ratio, capex-revenue-ratio, reinvestment-rate (which now uses annualRnD via Bug #25 fix) silently compute year-mismatched ratios. Worse for cyclicals where capex spiked in one year.
findings.suggested_fix: Make mapFTSToAnnual also push null placeholders for null-rev rows (preserve row count). OR have _ftsExtractByYear filter to align with mapFTSToAnnual's chosen subset (requires returning year tags or a row mask).

findings.title: _priceOnlyUpdate writes snapshot non-atomically — corrupts file on SIGKILL
findings.description: The price-only fast path reads existing.json, mutates several fields, then calls fs.writeFileSync(fp, JSON.stringify(...)) directly. There is no tmp+rename pattern. This is exactly the F-DP-002 / F-DP-028 issue but in the price-only update path which the previous audit overlooked. Tag 166 added this path AFTER F-DP-002 was filed (which only fixed the main snapshot write and manifest writes). Since the price-only path runs for tickers with <7-day-old snapshots — i.e., the majority of the universe — this is the most-frequently-executed snapshot write in the codebase.
findings.mechanism: fs.writeFileSync truncates the target file at open, then streams the JSON body. SIGKILL between truncate and end-of-write (or disk-full mid-write) leaves a zero-byte or partially-written file. Downstream JSON.parse throws, the ticker has no usable snapshot until the next full pull cycle, and the prune-watchlist heuristics may then mark it as stale-no-data.
findings.reproducer: kill -9 pull-yahoo while it's inside the price-only loop. With ~80% of pulls being price-only fast-path under Tag 166, ~80% probability of catching a write mid-flight at any given SIGKILL moment.
findings.impact: Snapshot corruption rate is multiplied vs the main-path write because price-only runs far more often. The Verify Pull Coverage gate then sees n_ok=correct but downstream readers fail on the corrupted JSON. Compound risk: corrupted snapshot has stale meta.asOf (preserved from prior write attempt before truncate? actually no, truncate is immediate — meta.asOf is gone entirely).
findings.suggested_fix: Use tmp+rename. Already applied at lines 896-898 (delisted-flag write) and 952-958 (manifest write); apply the same pattern here: `fs.writeFileSync(fp+'.tmp', body); fs.renameSync(fp+'.tmp', fp);`.

findings.title: refresh-fx.js writes fx-rates.json non-atomically — pull-yahoo may load partial JSON
findings.description: scripts/refresh-fx.js line 107: `fs.writeFileSync(outPath, JSON.stringify(out, null, 2))` directly writes fx-rates.json without tmp+rename. If the refresh-fx workflow step is killed (CI cancellation, runner OOM) mid-write, the file is left truncated. pull-yahoo's loadFx IIFE wraps the read in try/catch and silently falls back to FX_FALLBACK on parse error — so the failure is invisible at next pull-yahoo run. The whole snapshot universe then converts with the hardcoded 2024 fallback rates (already flagged stale by F-DP-024).
findings.mechanism: Standard writeFileSync atomicity gap. The CI workflow runs refresh-fx as a separate step before pull-yahoo; if scheduling interleaves them or the runner is preempted, fx-rates.json on the next pull is unreadable. pull-yahoo's loadFx catches parse error and only logs `fx-rates.json load failed` to stdout — no GitHub workflow failure surfaces.
findings.reproducer: kill the refresh-fx process mid-write; subsequent pull-yahoo loadFx sees corrupted JSON, silently uses 2024-vintage FX_FALLBACK. 16% conversion error for EUR-denominated stocks goes undetected.
findings.impact: Silent fallback to stale FX rates for the entire universe during one or more daily cycles until refresh-fx runs cleanly again. Compounds with F-DP-024.
findings.suggested_fix: Use tmp+rename in refresh-fx: write to fx-rates.json.tmp, then renameSync. Also: pull-yahoo loadFx should log WARN (not just plain log) and set a process-level flag exposed in the manifest if fallback is used.

findings.title: FX failed[] currencies retain old rates — pull-yahoo never consults the failed list
findings.description: refresh-fx.js was patched (F-DP-010) to track per-currency lastSuccessAt/lastAttemptAt and only update top-level fetchedAt on at least one success. But the bug F-DP-010 originally identified — that pull-yahoo doesn't consult `failed[]` — is NOT fixed in pull-yahoo's loadFx (lines 98-116). pull-yahoo merges `raw.rates` into FX_TO_USD unconditionally, so a currency that has been failing for 30 days but had a successful pull 60 days ago STILL has its 60-day-old rate served as 'live'. The currencyMeta tracking is dead documentation as far as pull-yahoo is concerned.
findings.mechanism: Line 110: `FX_TO_USD = Object.assign({}, FX_FALLBACK, raw.rates)` — raw.rates always includes the seeded old value for failed currencies (see refresh-fx.js line 69: `const rates = Object.assign({ USD: 1.0 }, existing)`). pull-yahoo never inspects raw.failed or raw.currencyMeta. Currencies persistently failing (e.g. CNY blocked from Yahoo) silently use whatever the last successful rate was, indefinitely.
findings.reproducer: Run refresh-fx with one currency hardcoded to always fail (e.g., simulate `fetchFXRate('JPY') === null`). raw.rates.JPY retains old value, fetchedAt=today (if any other succeeded), pull-yahoo sees fresh fx-rates.json and uses stale JPY rate.
findings.impact: Stale FX for failing currencies goes undetected for weeks/months. Most likely affects emerging-market currencies (TRY, IDR, THB) where Yahoo data can be flaky.
findings.suggested_fix: In pull-yahoo loadFx, after parsing raw, iterate raw.currencyMeta and drop any rate whose lastSuccessAt is >FX_STALE_DAYS old (force fxConversionFailed=true downstream). Alternative: in refresh-fx, write rates[c] = null when staleness exceeds threshold so pull-yahoo's `FX_TO_USD[c]==null` check at line 180 fires.

findings.title: Skipped-mcap path deletes snapshot but leaves stale fundamentals-cache for 28 days
findings.description: When a stock's marketCap falls below MIN_MCAP (1B USD), the snapshot file is unlinked at line 828-830 and the legacy-name variant at 832-835. But the corresponding fundamentals-cache/<ticker>.json file is NOT removed. The FTS cache TTL is 28 days. If the same ticker later climbs back above $1B mcap and gets a full pull, the cached FTS payload from up to 28 days ago is reused — meaning the new snapshot is built with current-day price/mcap but month-old fundamentals.
findings.mechanism: Lines 718-738 check cachePath = fundamentals-cache/<safeFilename(ticker)>.json with TTL=28 days. The skipped-mcap branch at 822-839 deletes snapshots but not the cache. Tickers cycling around the $1B boundary (e.g. small-cap technology with volatile mcap) end up with mismatched price-vs-fundamentals data on every re-entry.
findings.reproducer: (1) Pull ticker XYZ when mcap=$1.1B → snapshot + cache written. (2) Mcap drops to $0.9B → snapshot deleted, cache stays. (3) 20 days later mcap recovers to $1.2B → full pull starts, cache reused (age<28d), new snapshot has fresh price + 20-day-old fundamentals.
findings.impact: Methods consuming annual/quarterly series operate on stale fundamentals while showing 'fresh' meta.asOf. Quality grader sees ample data and rates it A, but data is internally inconsistent. Affects small-cap candidates that frequently bounce around the $1B threshold.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `fs.unlinkSync(cachePath)` in the skipped-mcap branch (with try/catch). Also unlink legacy-name cache file. Same fix should apply to the not-found branch where the snapshot is marked delisted.

findings.title: Price-only update preserves stale _quality.missingFields/nanRatio but nulls the grade
findings.description: F-DQ-009 partially addressed price-only mode by setting `existing._quality.grade = null`. But _quality.missingFields, _quality.nanRatio, and _quality.computedAt are NOT reset. If a downstream consumer (e.g. score-aggregator, dashboard) checks `_quality.nanRatio < 0.30` to gate methods, it sees the stale value from the prior full pull — which may no longer reflect today's marketCap value (especially if mcap moved enough that the snapshot now fails the $1B floor but is kept because the mcap filter only runs in the full-pull branch).
findings.mechanism: Price-only path only nulls `grade`. _quality.nanRatio etc are preserved. Score-aggregator code reading `nanRatio` (vs reading `grade`) bypasses the F-DQ-009 staleness signal.
findings.reproducer: Ticker has _quality={grade:'A', nanRatio:0.05, missingFields:[]} at full pull. Day 3 price-only update runs, only grade→null. score-aggregator code path checking nanRatio reads 0.05 (stale; price may have moved 30% but data quality is unchanged from the full pull, which is technically OK — but `missingFields` could be stale if the snapshot has changed shape).
findings.impact: Misleading data-quality state in price-only mode. Methods that gate on nanRatio rather than grade get falsely-fresh quality signal. Low-impact unless many methods use nanRatio directly.
findings.suggested_fix: Either clear the whole _quality block on price-only updates (set to null), or rerun gradeSnapshot on the modified existing snapshot since price/mcap are critical fields. Better: rerun gradeSnapshot — it's cheap.

findings.title: Yahoo screener exchange-page errors silently swallowed — universe shrinks invisibly
findings.description: fetchExchangePage wraps yf.screener in try/catch and returns []. No error logging, no retry, no flag in output. A persistent 429 or schema change at Yahoo's screener endpoint silently empties the entire exchange's contribution. The custom-screener pulls (lines 196-221) interpret empty as 'no more pages' and break the pagination loop. The universe-cap logic at 273-292 sees a smaller universe and the cap may not even trigger, masking the underlying coverage loss.
findings.mechanism: Line 136-138: `catch (e) { return []; }`. Line 199: `if (quotes.length === 0) { pageEmpty = true; break; }`. A 429 on page 0 of an exchange terminates that exchange's pagination after a single failed attempt with no diagnostic. Compared to F-DP-005 which fixed FTS partial-failure tracking, the screener path has no equivalent surface signal.
findings.reproducer: Mock yf.screener to throw '429 Too Many Requests' on FRA exchange. Run refresh-universe — Frankfurt-listed tickers disappear from universe silently. No warning in stdout.
findings.impact: Coverage loss for one or more exchanges per refresh cycle. Without alerting, operators won't notice until methods filtering by region show zero tickers from a region.
findings.suggested_fix: Log error in fetchExchangePage's catch (with exchange code + offset). Track per-exchange counts and emit a final summary; alert if any exchange returns <50% of prior run's count. Add retry-with-backoff on 429.

findings.title: insiderActivity.clusterBuys90d is identical to buyCount90d — not a cluster count
findings.description: Line 360 emits `{ clusterBuys90d: buyCount, buyCount90d: buyCount, ... }`. The two fields are literally the same value. A 'cluster buy' in insider-activity literature means multiple DISTINCT insiders buying within a tight window — it requires deduplication by filer name. The current code just counts total open-market purchase transactions, which double-counts a single insider's split-lot orders and entirely misses the 'multiple insiders' signal. Any method consuming `clusterBuys90d` (per Tag 137) gets garbage semantics dressed as 'cluster' data.
findings.mechanism: txns loop at lines 342-359 counts transaction rows that match purchase regex. No unique-filer set. clusterBuys90d should be `uniqueBuyers.size`, not `buyCount`.
findings.reproducer: Find a ticker where one insider executed 5 separate purchases in the last 90 days (common during structured buys). buyCount90d=5, clusterBuys90d=5. The 'cluster' is actually 1 insider, not 5.
findings.impact: Methods using insiderActivity.clusterBuys90d as a 'multiple insiders bullish' signal misfire on single-insider repeated trades. False-positive bullish signal.
findings.suggested_fix: Track a Set of filer names: `const buyers = new Set(); ... if (isOpenBuy) { buyers.add(tx.filerName || tx.ownerName || ''); buyCount++; }`. Emit `clusterBuys90d: buyers.size`. Drop the duplicated key or keep both for back-compat.

findings.title: writeManifestIncremental mutex causes silent flush drops at 100-ticker boundaries
findings.description: F-DP-012 was 'fixed' by adding a boolean mutex `_manifestWriting`. If two workers hit `myIdx % 100 === 0` near-simultaneously (e.g. myIdx=100 and myIdx=200), the second call sees `_manifestWriting=true` and silently returns without writing AND without retrying. Combined with how myIdx increments — only specific indices (100, 200, 300...) trigger a flush — there is no makeup write. A SIGKILL between two close-spaced flushes can leave the manifest reflecting only one of the two intended boundaries.
findings.mechanism: Lines 612-614: `if (_manifestWriting) return;`. Then `_manifestWriting = true; ...; finally { _manifestWriting = false; }`. While writing is in progress (~10ms typical writeFileSync), Worker B with myIdx=200 enters writeManifestFn(), sees flag, returns without scheduling a retry. The 200-boundary flush is silently lost.
findings.reproducer: Highly time-dependent: PULL_CONCURRENCY=20 with fast tickers — workers can finish 100 tickers in <1s, two flush boundaries overlap. Visible via timestamps in _manifest.json lagging behind actual progress.
findings.impact: Manifest staleness vs actual progress. Mid-run SIGKILL hits a manifest several hundred tickers behind reality. The Verify Pull Coverage gate may then incorrectly judge the partial run.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) make the mutex queue: if currently writing, defer the flush to fire after the current write completes (via a pending flag); or (b) accept the race but ALSO flush opportunistically from a setInterval timer.

findings.title: Price-only path overwrites price.currency with Yahoo's live value — loses GBp/GBpence distinction
findings.description: Line 668: `existing.price.currency = q.currency || existing.price.currency`. The previous full pull may have stored existing.price.currency='GBp' (pence) when the snapshot was originally pulled. After _convertSnapshotToUSD, meta.reportingCurrency='USD' but price.currency was left at its raw original value. On price-only update, q.currency may be 'GBP' or 'GBp' depending on Yahoo's response that day — these flip occasionally. The snapshot ends up with inconsistent currency labels between meta.reportingCurrency='USD', meta.reportingCurrencyOriginal='GBp', and price.currency=(whatever Yahoo said today).
findings.mechanism: Yahoo's currency field for UK stocks alternates between 'GBp' (pence) and 'GBP' (pounds) on different requests for the same symbol. The price-only path persists this flip into existing.price.currency, breaking the invariant that price.currency matches meta.reportingCurrencyOriginal.
findings.reproducer: Pull a UK pence-denominated stock (e.g. LLOY.L). Inspect existing.price.currency=GBp. Run price-only update; Yahoo returns currency='GBP' that day. Now existing.price.currency='GBP' but the price value is still scaled to USD via GBp/100*rate — internal contradiction.
findings.impact: Any downstream code that re-derives USD from existing.price * lookup(currency) would double-scale or mis-scale. Currently no code does this directly, but the field is misleading for diagnostics and breaks the GBp→GBP invariant downstream methods may rely on.
findings.suggested_fix: Don't overwrite price.currency in the price-only path — it's a fundamental property of the snapshot. Only overwrite price.regularMarketPrice. Add an assertion: if q.currency !== existing.price.currency (case-insensitive), log a WARN.

findings.title: FTS field-name fallbacks miss Yahoo's snake_case variants (post-Nov-2024 schema drift)
findings.description: Yahoo's fundamentalsTimeSeries response has used both camelCase ('totalRevenue') and snake_case ('total_revenue') field names depending on the period/endpoint version. The fallback lists in mapFTSToAnnual / mapFTSToBalance / mapFTSToQuarterly cover only camelCase + PascalCase variants. Examples: `_ftsValue(r, 'totalRevenue', 'TotalRevenue')` does NOT try 'total_revenue'. Similarly for 'operatingIncome' / 'cashAndCashEquivalents' / 'capitalExpenditure'. If Yahoo's API gateway resolves to a node emitting snake_case (observed during regional load-balancing), all FTS fields read as null and the entire FTS payload is silently empty.
findings.mechanism: Yahoo's internal services on the fundamentalsTimeSeries endpoint sometimes mix casing. yahoo-finance2 normalizes most fields to camelCase but not all in the rolled-up FTS response. _ftsValue iterates only the listed keys.
findings.reproducer: Difficult to deterministically reproduce since it depends on which Yahoo edge node serves the request. Surfaced symptom: a ticker reports ftsPartial=true with ALL four modules empty (annualFin/annualCash/annualBs/quarterlyFin all length 0) despite no exception thrown — the rows arrived but field-name lookup failed.
findings.impact: Silent data loss for affected tickers. Cache stores empty payload with _ftsPartial=true, partial TTL is 24h, but a sticky edge-node assignment can keep the ticker in the failing state across multiple pull cycles.
findings.suggested_fix: Expand fallback lists to include snake_case: 'total_revenue', 'operating_income', 'cash_and_cash_equivalents', 'capital_expenditure', 'free_cash_flow', etc. Even cheaper: convert each row's keys to a Map<lowerCaseStrippedUnderscores, value> once and look up case-insensitively.

findings.title: FX_FALLBACK lookup is exact-match; pull-yahoo's _convertToUSD ignores fxKey normalization for GBp
findings.description: _convertToUSD (line 117-122) is a separate helper using `FX_TO_USD[currency.toUpperCase()]`. It does NOT handle the GBp→GBP pence normalization that _convertSnapshotToUSD does at line 177. If any code path calls _convertToUSD with 'GBp', it returns null (because FX_TO_USD doesn't have a 'GBP' entry under 'GBP'? actually FX_TO_USD has 'GBP' but not 'GBp' — `currency.toUpperCase()` makes 'GBP' so lookup succeeds, but the pence-to-pounds /100 division is NOT applied). The value gets returned scaled by GBP rate but still in pence units → 100x too small.
findings.mechanism: Line 119: `FX_TO_USD[currency.toUpperCase()]` — 'GBp'.toUpperCase()='GBP' → rate found, applied → result is 100x too small for pence-denominated values. _convertToUSD is currently called nowhere in pull-yahoo.js (it's dead code in the main flow), but it's exported via require — any future caller that uses it will silently produce wrong results for UK pence stocks.
findings.reproducer: Call `_convertToUSD(50.00, 'GBp')` from outside. Returns 50 * 1.27 = 63.5 USD. Correct value is 50/100*1.27 = 0.635 USD. 100x error.
findings.impact: Dead code today, latent footgun. Any future refactor that uses this helper will silently mis-scale pence-denominated values.
findings.suggested_fix: Either delete _convertToUSD (it's unused), or make it handle the GBp/GBX case identical to _convertSnapshotToUSD at line 177-178.

findings.title: wikipedia-indices NOT_TICKERS set blocks legitimate tickers like SA/NV/AG
findings.description: NOT_TICKERS set (F-DP-018 mitigation) includes 'SA', 'NV', 'AG', 'PE', 'PB', 'PS', 'EV' — these are also valid ticker symbols on some exchanges. SA = Seabridge Gold (TSE), NV = NovaGold (preferred), AG = First Majestic Silver, PE = Parker Drilling (historically), EV = Eaton Vance. Filtering them out via NOT_TICKERS produces false-negatives in Wikipedia-sourced discovery, particularly for cross-listed Canadian/European companies.
findings.mechanism: Line 78-80 in wikipedia-indices.js: `if (NOT_TICKERS.has(clean)) continue;`. If wikitext for FTSE 100 contains a row with 'AG' or 'SA' as a constituent reference (e.g. Anglo American AG), it's silently skipped.
findings.reproducer: Inspect Wikipedia 'List_of_S%26P_500_companies' wikitext — any company with a 2-letter ticker in NOT_TICKERS list gets dropped. AG = First Majestic Silver is NYSE-listed and could appear in cross-index tables.
findings.impact: Minor coverage gap in Wikipedia-discovered tickers. Affects only 2-letter tickers overlapping with NOT_TICKERS entries (~5-10 affected tickers globally).
findings.suggested_fix: Make NOT_TICKERS context-aware — only filter when the wikitext cell is not in a recognized 'Symbol' or 'Ticker' column. Or remove 2-letter false-positives from the set and rely on length>=3 heuristic. Trade-off documented.

findings.title: Discovery http.get redirect follows without consuming original response body
findings.file: discovery/finnhub.js, sec-tickers.js, nasdaq-all.js, otc-markets.js, nasdaq-api.js, wikipedia-indices.js
findings.description: All discovery modules' get() helper has the pattern: `if (res.statusCode === 301 || res.statusCode === 302) { return get(res.headers.location).then(resolve).catch(reject); }`. The original response body is not consumed (no `res.resume()` or `res.destroy()`), which can leak the underlying socket — Node's http agent keeps the connection in 'reading' state waiting for the body to drain. On a long-running process making many redirected requests this can exhaust the socket pool.
findings.mechanism: Per Node.js docs, when a response is received but not consumed, the connection cannot be released to the agent's pool. With keepAlive=true (default agent behavior), this leaves hanging sockets until GC. Long-running refresh-universe (~5-10 min) making dozens of redirected calls per discovery source can leak 20+ sockets.
findings.reproducer: Force one of the discovery URLs to return 301 and observe Node's `_http_agent` socket count. Trace via `--inspect` and inspect agent.sockets.
findings.impact: Memory and FD leak per discovery run. Bounded by total redirected URLs (~50-100 across all sources). Not catastrophic but accumulates in long-lived CI runner processes.
findings.suggested_fix: Before recursing: `res.resume();` or `res.destroy();` to drain the original response. Standard pattern: `res.resume(); return get(res.headers.location)...`

findings.title: Cache pollution: failed _quality grade exception traps return D-grade but snapshot still written
findings.description: Line 842-843: `try { canonical._quality = gradeSnapshot(canonical); } catch (e) { canonical._quality = { grade: 'D', nanRatio: 1.0, missingFields: ['<grade-error>'], computedAt: ... }; }`. The fallback assigns D-grade with `missingFields: ['<grade-error>']` to ANY exception. Then the snapshot is written to disk and the manifest counts this as `status: 'ok'` (line 867 runs unconditionally after this try/catch). Downstream code reading _quality.grade='D' filters this ticker out — equivalent to silent loss. No log is emitted with the actual exception detail.
findings.mechanism: Most gradeSnapshot exceptions would come from malformed snapshot shape (e.g. canonical.metrics is null). The catch swallows the message — operators don't know if it's a data-quality grading bug or a real snapshot defect.
findings.reproducer: Pass a snapshot where canonical.metrics is null. gradeSnapshot internally accesses `metrics.revenueTTM`, throws TypeError. The catch traps; snapshot is written with _quality.grade='D'. No log line, no errClass.
findings.impact: Silent grade-D for snapshots with grading bugs. Score-aggregator drops them. Failure looks like data-quality issue when it's actually a code path bug.
findings.suggested_fix: Log the actual exception in the catch block: `_log('WARN', 'grade-snapshot threw for ' + stock.ticker + ': ' + e.message)`. Optionally attach exception.message to _quality.missingFields for forensics.

findings.title: Watchlist write in refresh-universe.js is non-atomic
findings.description: Line 327: `fs.writeFileSync(args.out, JSON.stringify(wlRaw, null, 2))`. Direct writeFileSync on watchlist.json (typically the same path as input). If the refresh-universe process is killed mid-write (CI cancellation), watchlist.json is corrupted. Since refresh-universe runs daily before pull-yahoo, a corrupted watchlist breaks the entire pipeline for that cycle.
findings.mechanism: writeFileSync truncates target, then streams body. SIGKILL between truncate and end-of-write leaves zero-byte or partial JSON. Next pull-yahoo run's `JSON.parse(fs.readFileSync(args.watchlist, ...))` throws, pull-yahoo exits with code 1, no snapshots updated. Whole day's pull is lost.
findings.reproducer: kill refresh-universe during the final writeFileSync. Inspect watchlist.json — partial JSON. pull-yahoo aborts.
findings.impact: Pipeline-blocking corruption risk for the watchlist file. Lower probability than per-snapshot writes but higher blast radius.
findings.suggested_fix: Use tmp+rename: `fs.writeFileSync(args.out + '.tmp', body); fs.renameSync(args.out + '.tmp', args.out);`.

findings.title: Worker pool error in _log/console.warn after results.push leaves orphan results entry
findings.description: processOne pushes to `results` array at multiple points (line 692 price-only, 867 main). If any console operation or _log call between push and function return throws (e.g. stdout closed, EPIPE), the function exits via .catch in the worker loop. The push has already happened — results contains an entry for the ticker — but no snapshot was written (or was written with partial state). The manifest then reports n_ok including this entry while the snapshot file may be missing or malformed.
findings.mechanism: Lines 692/867: results.push happens before the function returns. If _log at line 693 or 868 throws (rare but possible with EPIPE), the worker's `.catch(e => _log('WARN', ...))` at line 924 catches the exception but the results entry persists. The snapshot may or may not have been written depending on which _log threw.
findings.reproducer: Close stdout pipe externally (kill parent process keeping pipe open) during a pull — subsequent console.log calls throw EPIPE. Worker continues but state is inconsistent.
findings.impact: Manifest n_ok overstates actual snapshot count. Verify Pull Coverage gate passes on inflated count.
findings.suggested_fix: Move results.push to the very end of processOne, after the snapshot write succeeds. Or push BEFORE the write but use a single atomic 'commit' that writes both snapshot and a marker file.

findings.title: quoteSummaryWithRetry does not retry on timeout class — only rate-limit
findings.description: Comment at line 593-594 implies both rate-limit AND timeout trigger retries: `if ((isRateLimit || isTimeout) && attempt < DELAYS.length)`. But the timeout detection on line 592-593 checks for `e.constructor.name === 'timeout'` (lowercase). yahoo-finance2's timeout error class is typically named 'TimeoutError' (PascalCase) or thrown as a plain Error via _withTimeout (line 539 throws `new Error('ETIMEDOUT after...')`). The lowercase 'timeout' check rarely matches; the function instead relies on the regex `/timeout|ETIMEDOUT/i` to catch _withTimeout's error message — but on classes from yahoo-finance2 itself (e.g. AbortError, TimeoutError), neither check matches and the timeout is re-thrown without retry.
findings.mechanism: yahoo-finance2 may throw `new TimeoutError('Operation timed out')` from its internal axios layer. constructor.name='TimeoutError' (not 'timeout'). Message 'Operation timed out' matches `/timeout/i` only by the 'timed out' substring — the message also contains 'Operation', not 'timeout' as a word. The regex `/timeout|ETIMEDOUT/i` requires the literal 'timeout' which 'timed out' (past tense) does not match. F-DP-003 noted this partially but the fix added isTimeout detection that fails to match yf2's own timeout class.
findings.reproducer: Force yahoo-finance2 to throw TimeoutError with message 'Operation timed out'. Inspect: isTimeout=false (no exact 'timeout' substring), no retry. Ticker fails after first attempt.
findings.impact: Lower retry success rate for yf2-raised timeouts. Fail-ratio inflated by single-attempt timeout failures that one retry would have rescued.
findings.suggested_fix: Broaden the regex: `/timeout|timed out|ETIMEDOUT|ESOCKETTIMEDOUT/i`. Also check the error name for 'Timeout' (case-insensitive substring).

findings.title: wikipedia parser splits on /\|\-/  which also splits inside cell content with literal '|-'
findings.description: Line 65: `const rows = wikitext.split(/\|\-/);`. Wikipedia row separator is '|-' but cell content can include the literal sequence (e.g. in piped wiki-links `[[Page|-text-]]` or in template arguments). The split conflates real row separators with content-internal '|-' sequences, causing spurious row boundaries. Combined with the cell-splitting at line 68 (`row.split(/\|+/)`), partial rows can match ticker regex on stray fragments.
findings.mechanism: Real-world wikitext from S&P 500 page contains template invocations like {{|...|-...|}} that include '|-' as part of template-internal syntax. The split misidentifies these as row boundaries, fragmenting one row into multiple pseudo-rows.
findings.reproducer: Run fetchIndexTickers on a page with templated cells. Compare extracted ticker count vs Wikipedia's actual table row count.
findings.impact: Possible spurious tickers extracted. F-DP-018 mitigates with NOT_TICKERS set, but unknown fragments still slip through.
findings.suggested_fix: Use a real wikitext parser (e.g. mwparserfromhell-equivalent for JS) or restrict the split to line-start '|-' only: `/\n\s*\|\-/`. Even simpler: switch to MediaWiki action=parse with format=json and parse the rendered HTML instead of wikitext.

findings.title: discovery/finnhub.js exchange map omits Madrid, Brussels, Lisbon, Vienna — Euronext coverage incomplete
findings.description: EXCHANGES map covers US, L, T, HK, F, PA, MI, ST, TO, AX, KS, TW, SP, SW, OL, CO, HE. Missing: MC (Madrid), BR (Brussels), LS (Lisbon), VI (Vienna), IR (Dublin), WA (Warsaw), AT (Athens). Most are part of the EU regional discovery set in pull-yahoo (REGION_BY_CURRENCY maps EUR=EU) and would be valuable for breadth. NASDAQ-Trader and SEC sources are US-only, so Finnhub is the main discovery channel for these exchanges.
findings.mechanism: Coverage gap: tickers listed only on Madrid (e.g. .MC suffix) are not discovered via Finnhub. They may be picked up by Yahoo's custom-exchange screener (refresh-universe.js EXCHANGE_CODES) but only if Yahoo's screener has them.
findings.reproducer: Run refresh-universe, inspect added tickers — count per suffix. Madrid (.MC), Brussels (.BR), Vienna (.VI), Dublin (.IR) underrepresented.
findings.impact: Reduced universe breadth for smaller European exchanges. Affects ~50-100 tickers per missing exchange.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend EXCHANGES map with MC→.MC, BR→.BR, LS→.LS, VI→.VI, IR→.IR, WA→.WA, AT→.AT. Verify Yahoo Finance accepts these suffixes (most do).

findings.title: Failed FX rate keeps last-known-good rate but pull-yahoo's loadFx accepts the file as fresh
findings.description: refresh-fx.js's F-DP-010 fix only updates the top-level `fetchedAt` when `anySuccess === true` (at least one currency succeeded). pull-yahoo.js loadFx (line 106) accepts the file as fresh if `ageDays <= FX_STALE_DAYS=14`. The problem: if a single non-USD currency (say EUR) succeeds but all 9 EM currencies fail, fetchedAt is updated to today and pull-yahoo sees the file as 'fresh' — but most non-major-currency rates may be weeks old. The 14-day staleness check is too coarse to detect this skew.
findings.mechanism: Line 100 in refresh-fx.js: `anySuccess = CURRENCIES.some(c => currencyMeta[c]?.lastSuccessAt === nowIso)`. As long as ONE of ~30 currencies succeeds, fetchedAt=today. In pull-yahoo loadFx, FX_STALE_DAYS check is against fetchedAt only, not against per-currency lastSuccessAt.
findings.reproducer: Mock refresh-fx so only EUR succeeds, all EM currencies fail. fx-rates.json has fetchedAt=today but currencyMeta.TRY.lastSuccessAt=30 days ago. pull-yahoo loads file, FX_TO_USD.TRY uses 30-day-old rate, no warning, no fxConversionFailed flag.
findings.impact: Silent use of weeks-old FX rates for currencies whose live-fetch is failing. Especially dangerous during currency crises (TRY in 2024 moved 30% in months).
findings.suggested_fix: In pull-yahoo loadFx, when loading raw, also check raw.currencyMeta[c].lastSuccessAt per currency. If any individual lastSuccessAt is >FX_STALE_DAYS old, drop that currency from FX_TO_USD (force fxConversionFailed in snapshot).

findings.title: FTS cache writeFileSync is not atomic — concurrent workers writing same cache file possible
findings.description: Lines 770-777: cache write uses `fs.writeFileSync(cachePath, JSON.stringify(...))` directly. While each worker processes a distinct ticker (so unique cachePath), the not-found branch (line 887) may unlink + rewrite the same file from a different code path. More relevantly: if a previous run's stale cache file is being read at the same moment a new run is writing to the same path, the new run's reader (line 728) gets a partially-truncated JSON and parse-error silently drops the cache (line 737: empty catch). The result: cache is rebuilt unnecessarily, costing 4 extra Yahoo HTTP calls.
findings.mechanism: Concurrent pull-yahoo processes (rare but possible during overlapping CI runs) racing on the cache file. The reader at line 727-737 wraps in try/catch and silently treats parse error as cache-miss, falling through to a full FTS fetch. This is benign but wastes API quota.
findings.reproducer: Launch two pull-yahoo processes targeting overlapping tickers. With 10-min duration each, overlap window for any given ticker is small but non-zero.
findings.impact: Wasted FTS API calls during concurrent runs. Worse: cache file can be left truncated until next pull rewrites it.
findings.suggested_fix: Use tmp+rename for cache writes too. Standard pattern.

findings.title: mapYahooToCanonical insider startDate parsing breaks for Date-typed values
findings.description: Line 343: `const ts = tx.startDate && (typeof tx.startDate === 'number' ? tx.startDate * 1000 : new Date(tx.startDate).getTime());`. yahoo-finance2 typically returns startDate as a Date instance (because it normalizes Unix timestamps). `typeof Date === 'object'`, so the ternary falls through to `new Date(tx.startDate).getTime()`. `new Date(<Date instance>)` does work — but `new Date(<Date instance>).getTime()` is equivalent to `tx.startDate.getTime()`. OK. BUT: the leading guard `tx.startDate && ...` returns the falsy value when startDate is missing — and `0` (Unix epoch) is also falsy. Any insider with epoch-zero startDate (rare but observable for malformed Yahoo data) is silently dropped. More concerning: if startDate is the string '0' or numeric 0, the guard returns the boolean 0/falsy, which then falls into `ts && ts < cutoffMs` → falsy, skip — that's correct.
findings.mechanism: Date 1970-01-01 falsiness via Unix-epoch zero. Not a real issue for modern transactions. The bigger concern: line 343 doesn't handle `tx.startDate` being an ISO string like '2025-03-15T00:00:00.000Z' correctly under the `typeof === 'number'` check — falls through to `new Date(ISO).getTime()` which works. OK.
findings.reproducer: Construct yahoo response with `transactions: [{ startDate: '2025-03-15', transactionText: 'Purchase' }]`. Verify ts is correctly parsed.
findings.impact: Currently no real bug, but the date-handling logic is fragile. Future Yahoo schema change (e.g. milliseconds vs seconds) could silently misclassify all timestamps.
findings.suggested_fix: Normalize startDate via a helper that handles number-as-seconds, number-as-ms, Date instance, and ISO string. Add a sanity check: reject ts > Date.now() or ts < (Date.now() - 10 * 365 * 86400 * 1000).

findings.title: fetchFundamentalsTS uses 4 sequential awaits (already documented in fixes-for-pull-yahoo.md)
findings.description: fetchFundamentalsTS at lines 420-432 issues four sequential awaits for fundamentalsTimeSeries (annual financials, quarterly financials, annual cash-flow, annual balance-sheet). These are independent HTTP calls and could fire via Promise.all to reduce wall-clock time by ~3/4. Documented in fixes-for-pull-yahoo.md as F-PF-012 but not yet applied. Worth flagging again since it's directly impacting throughput (each ticker spends ~4-6s on FTS, dominating total pull time).
findings.mechanism: Sequential awaits serialize HTTP latency. With 4 calls × 1.5s average = 6s per ticker just for FTS. Parallelizing brings it down to ~1.5-2s.
findings.reproducer: Time fetchFundamentalsTS for a typical ticker. Measure sum of 4 sequential calls vs Promise.all of the same 4 calls.
findings.impact: Pull duration is ~3x longer than necessary. For 11k tickers at 6s/ticker concurrency-10, total pull = 110 min; with parallelization could be ~40 min. Trivial fix, large speedup.
findings.suggested_fix: See fixes-for-pull-yahoo.md F-PF-012 — use Promise.all with safe() wrapper for per-call try/catch preservation.
